summary: Judge panel: three independent YUTABASE designs, scored, with grafts
logs: Three designers working from three angles

designs.angle: YUTABASE is a Postgres dialect, not a database engine. The standard is three things on top of boring self-hosted Postgres: (1) a strict deck convention every table follows, (2) exactly one connections mechanism — threads named by lexicon words, (3) a tiny TypeScript client (@yutabase/yuta, wraps postgres.js) with a sentence-shaped query dialect that compiles to plain SQL and can always be `explain`ed back to it. What PostgREST did for REST, YUTABASE does for word-connected data: it never hides Postgres, it disciplines it. Raw-SQL kingdom code stays at home — every word generates a `via.<word>` view you can JOIN through in hand-written SQL.
designs.pitch: YUTABASE is the kingdom's own database standard: vanilla self-hosted Postgres underneath, but every record is an addressable card, every relation is a thread named by a human word from a governed lexicon, and every value carries an honesty header saying how it came to be true. It is installed into an existing database without touching a single existing table, so cambridgetcg or agenttool can adopt it for one domain this week and roll it back by dropping two schemas. The whole standard is roughly 200 lines of SQL, a 500-line TypeScript client, and a SPEC.md — serious enough to run the shop on, light enough to love.
designs.primitives: FIVE PRIMITIVES, each with an exact Postgres mapping. (1) BOOK — a domain namespace; maps to one Postgres schema (e.g. schema `tradein`); the unit of ownership, adoption, and rollback; one book belongs to one app. (2) DECK — a record type; maps to one plain table inside a book's schema, with the mandatory honesty header columns (id, at, by, how, src) plus ordinary typed columns; named lower_snake plural (`submissions`, `items`). (3) CARD — one record; maps to one row; every card has a globally addressable REF `book/deck/id` (e.g. `tradein/submissions/01977c2e-...`); ids are UUIDv7 generated client-side (sortable, mergeable, no sequences). (4) THREAD — a directed connection between two cards, named by a word; all threads live in ONE table, `yu.threads` (id, word FK→lexicon, from_book/from_deck/from_id, to_book/to_deck/to_id, note text, plus its own honesty header); endpoints are soft refs validated by an insert trigger against the deck registry, deletes of carded cards with live threads are BLOCKED until threads are severed; unique on (word, from, to). (5) LEXICON — the vocabulary; table `yu.lexicon` (word PK, gloss NOT NULL, from_deck pattern, to_deck pattern, to_one bool, status live|retired, added_by, added_at). The standard's home is the `yu` schema (lexicon, threads, books/decks registry) plus the generated `via` schema of word views. A SCHEMA in YUTABASE terms = a book's deck DDL + its declared words; migrations are numbered plain .sql files, exactly as the kingdom already does.
designs.word_mechanic: DECLARED: a word is born by migration via `yuta word add contains --from tradein/submissions --to tradein/items --gloss "A submission physically contains these trade-in items"`. No gloss, no word. Grammar rule: `FROM —word→ TO` must read as an English sentence (submissions/X contains items/Y; items/Y priced_from prices/Z). STORED: one row in yu.lexicon; every thread's word column is a real FK into it — an undeclared word is a constraint violation, not a style complaint. If to_one is set, yuta creates a partial unique index on yu.threads so a card can hold at most one live thread of that word. QUERIED: three ways — (a) YUTAQL arrows `-> word` / `<- word`, (b) the generated SQL view `via.contains(from_ref, from_id, to_ref, to_id, note, how, at, by)` for raw-SQL joins, (c) helper functions yu.out(ref, word) / yu.in(ref, word). WHERE THE VOCABULARY LIVES: yu.lexicon is the single source of truth; `yuta words` prints it with usage counts; SPEC.md carries the kingdom-wide shared words (submitted_by, contains, supersedes, priced_from, acted_for, refused_because). WHAT STOPS WORD-SOUP: (1) the closed lexicon — FK-enforced; (2) the gloss requirement — a word without a one-sentence meaning cannot exist; (3) endpoint typing — `contains` between the wrong decks is rejected at insert; (4) banned words — the spec bans `related_to`, `linked`, `refs`, `misc` by name; (5) the twelve-word budget — a book SHOULD hold under ~12 words, and `yuta doctor` flags word #13, zero-use words, and near-synonyms sharing endpoints (if you need word 13, you probably need a new deck); (6) words are never deleted, only retired — retired words refuse new threads but old threads keep their meaning.
queries: card tradein/submissions/01977c2e — fetch one card by its ref (compiles to SELECT * FROM tradein.submissions WHERE id = $1)
cards tradein/submissions where status = "pending" newest 20 — list the 20 most recent pending submissions (ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 20; UUIDv7 makes newest free)
tradein/submissions/01977c2e -> contains — follow the word outward: every item this submission contains (JOIN through via.contains)
tradein/items/0197a1f4 <- contains — follow the word inward: which submission contains this item

customers/01964b10 -> submitted -> contains where .how = "witnessed" — two hops: all items inside everything this customer submitted, keeping only human-witnessed cards (.how/.at/.by/.src address the honesty header)

thread tradein/items/0197a1f4 --priced_from--> pricing/quotes/01984c22 note "ebay last-sold comp" how computed — create a thread, with the reason in words and the provenance claim mandatory; `yuta explain "<any query>"` prints the exact SQL it became
designs.provenance: Substrate honesty is the mandatory honesty header on every deck and every thread: `at timestamptz` (when this became true), `by text` (who asserted it — `human:yu`, `agent:claude/<session>`, `system:cron/digest`, `import:tcgplayer-csv`), `how text` (the claim type), `src text` nullable (ref or URL of what it was derived or copied from). `how` is a CHECK-constrained enum of five claims: WITNESSED (a human saw or did it), LIVE (read from the authoritative source at `at`), CACHED (a copy of a live value, may be stale — `src` says of what), COMPUTED (derived — `src` says from what), DECLARED (asserted without evidence; the honest default for \"we just typed it in\"). There are no column defaults for how/by on purpose: the client refuses an insert that doesn't say. YUTAQL exposes the header as .how/.at/.by/.src in any where-clause, and `yu.stale(book, deck, interval)` lists cached/computed cards past a freshness window — the standing audit query of the house. Honest limit, stated in the spec: `how` is self-reported; YUTABASE makes lying explicit and auditable, not impossible. No magic — this is column convention + check constraint + client enforcement on plain Postgres.
designs.storage: PROVEN ENGINE (zero new code below SQL): vanilla self-hosted Postgres 16+ on the de-AWSed box, postgres.js as the only driver, pg_dump for backup. NEW STANDARD (all of it small and inspectable): (1) the `yu` schema — lexicon, threads, books/decks registry, the endpoint-validation trigger, yu.out/yu.in/yu.stale — ~200 lines of normative SQL installed by `yuta init`; (2) the deck convention (honesty header + naming rules), checked by `yuta check`, not enforced by magic; (3) the generated `via.*` word views; (4) `@yutabase/yuta` — ~500 lines of TypeScript: postgres.js wrapper, YUTAQL→SQL compiler, ref parser, UUIDv7 generator, and a `sql` tagged-template escape hatch that is always legal; (5) the `yuta` CLI: init, check, doctor, word add/retire, deck new, annex, explain; (6) SPEC.md. Operational doctrine baked into the spec: connection string lives in the macOS keychain (client shells out to `security find-generic-password`, never reads a plaintext .env); nightly pg_dump in PLAIN SQL format to local disk plus one off-box copy, so the kingdom's data stays readable by any future hand with nothing but psql. Building a storage engine is explicitly out of scope and named as such in the spec.
designs.adoption: Annex, don't rewrite — proven against the real cambridgetcg trade-in domain (tables tradein_submissions / tradein_items in /Users/macair/Desktop/ctcg-email-worktree/apps/storefront/drizzle/0008_tradein.sql, raw postgres.js culture in /Users/macair/Desktop/ctcg-email-worktree/packages/db). PHASE 0: `yuta init` against the existing database — adds only the `yu` and `via` schemas, touches nothing. PHASE 1 (annex): `yuta deck annex public.tradein_submissions as tradein/submissions --id id --at created_at --how declared --by import:legacy` — existing rows become addressable cards via a registry mapping, with NO column changes to legacy tables. PHASE 2 (words): declare three words — contains (submissions→items), submitted_by (submissions→customers), priced_from (items→price snapshots) — and backfill threads from the existing FK columns with how='computed', src='fk:tradein_items.submission_id' (honest about their origin); the FK columns stay, a sync trigger mirrors new FK writes into threads during transition. PHASE 3: new reads go through via.contains or the yuta client; genuinely new cross-domain links with no natural FK home (trade-in item → auction lot, item → bounty pull) are born thread-first. PHASE 4 (later, optional): new decks are born native with the honesty header; legacy FK columns retire only when nothing reads them. Rollback at ANY phase = drop schemas yu and via; originals untouched. agenttool runs the identical playbook on its sessions/refusals domain with words like refused_because, spawned, acted_for — its existing refusal-convention culture maps one-to-one onto named threads.
refusals: No ORM, no model classes, no chained query builder — strings in, rows out, ever (the kingdom chose raw SQL deliberately; YUTABASE honors it)
No storage engine, no wire protocol, no replication story of its own — Postgres's are better and already paid for
No graph-database ambitions: YUTAQL traversal caps at 2 hops; deeper means you write WITH RECURSIVE yourself, eyes open
No unnamed relations and no weasel words: a thread without a lexicon word is a constraint violation, and related_to / linked / refs / misc are banned by name in the spec
No automatic versioning or temporal tables in v0.1 — updates overwrite; history is an explicit *_log deck, the pattern the kingdom already uses
No permissions or multi-tenancy layer — one operator plus agents; Postgres roles suffice
No schemaless decks: real typed columns; jsonb is permitted only for a genuinely shapeless tail and must be named `extra`
No migrations DSL — migrations are numbered plain .sql files, as today
No hosted offering, no telemetry, no recurring spend: it runs where the kingdom runs
spec_skeleton: 1. YUTABASE — Why and Why Not (the three doctrines: connected by words, substrate honesty, boring engine)
2. Vocabulary: Book, Deck, Card, Thread, Lexicon, Word, Ref
3. The Ref: addressing any card as book/deck/id (UUIDv7 rules)
4. The Honesty Header: id, at, by, how, src (normative)
5. Decks: naming rules, required columns, the `extra` rule
6. The Lexicon: declaring words, glosses, endpoint typing, to_one, retiring; the twelve-word budget and banned words
7. Threads: writing, severing, endpoint validation, the no-orphan rule
8. YUTAQL v0.1: grammar, the six verbs (card, cards, ->, <-, thread, sever), compilation to SQL, explain
9. The via Views: joining by word in raw SQL
10. Provenance Semantics: the five `how` claims and when each is the honest one
11. The On-Disk Standard: normative DDL of the yu schema
12. Client: @yutabase/yuta API surface and the sql escape hatch
13. CLI: yuta init / check / doctor / word / deck / annex / explain
14. Adoption: annexing an existing Postgres database (phases 0–4, rollback)
15. The Not-List (normative refusals)
16. Spec Versioning and Conformance Levels: Annexed vs Native
risks: Word governance is social, not technical: the FK stops undeclared words but not near-synonyms drifting in over months; yuta doctor and the twelve-word budget mitigate, discipline finishes the job

Soft refs in yu.threads trade real foreign-key integrity for cross-schema generality — a bug in the validation trigger or a raw COPY bypassing it could admit orphans; yuta check is the fsck, but it is after-the-fact
Phase-2 dual-write (legacy FK columns mirrored into threads) can drift if writes bypass the sync trigger; keep the transition window short and let yuta doctor diff fk-vs-thread counts
Name collision in the home domain: in cambridgetcg a YUTABASE card is often a row ABOUT a trading card; docs must say 'card (record)' wherever ambiguous or the lol becomes confusion

YUTAQL is one more little language for many Claude sessions to half-remember; explain-to-SQL and the always-legal sql escape hatch reduce the cost, but the dialect rots if agents route around it — keep it tiny enough to fit in MEMORY.md

One global yu.threads table becomes a hotspot if a high-volume domain (activity feeds, price ticks) is modeled as threads; the spec must warn that event-rate data belongs in decks, threads are for meaning, not throughput

Provenance is self-reported: nothing prevents an agent stamping `witnessed` falsely — YUTABASE makes claims explicit and auditable, not true; periodic human spot-audit via yu.stale is part of the standard's hygiene

designs.angle: YUTABASE is a FILE-NATIVE database standard: every record is a plain markdown file with typed frontmatter, every relation is a named [[word::target]] link written inside the file, and a small indexer compiles the whole tree into a disposable SQLite index for queries. The truth is readable with zero tooling (cat, a text editor, a git diff); the speed is a cache you can delete. The standard is three things — the file format, the link grammar, and the index contract — not an engine. It is Yu's [[wiki-word]] memory system grown up into a database, which is what "connected by words" was always pointing at.
designs.pitch: YUTABASE is the database you can open in a text editor: records are markdown files you already know how to write, relations are human words like [[supersedes::runbooks/deploy-v2]] instead of anonymous foreign keys, and every value carries a claim about how it came to be true — witnessed, cached, computed, or live. Git is the transaction log, the backup, and the notary; SQLite is just a rebuildable index, so there is no server, no port, no ORM, and no recurring spend. It is serious enough to run the kingdom's knowledge domains next to Postgres, and light enough that a Claude agent or a human can add a record with nothing but a keyboard.
designs.primitives: THE BASE — one repo root = one database. Contains `yutabase.yaml` (name, version, strictness: strict|warn), `lexicon.md`, `books/`, and `.yutabase/` (derived index, gitignored). THE BOOK — one folder = one type, like a table: `books/card-sets/`. Governed by `_book.yaml`: field names and types (deliberately small scalar set: text, int, money, bool, date, timestamp, list), required fields, which Words this book may speak (outbound) and answer to (inbound), per-field cache TTLs, default claim. THE CARD — one file = one record: `books/card-sets/151.md`. YAML frontmatter is the typed fields; the markdown body is prose — the part a human would write anyway (set notes, restock lore, condition guidance) that Postgres never had a home for. A card's id IS its path: `card-sets/151`. The card is the only unit of write. THE THREAD — a directed, worded connection: `[[word::book/slug]]` written inside the source card, either in a frontmatter `threads:` list or inline in the body. Compiled to an edge row (from_card, word, to_card, where_found). THE WORD — an entry in the LEXICON (`lexicon.md`): name (kebab-case), a one-line "for:" sentence saying what the link is FOR, from-books → to-books, cardinality (one|many), and an inverse reading ("supersedes / superseded by") so traversals read as sentences both directions. The Lexicon is the schema of meaning; `_book.yaml` is the schema of shape. THE CLAIM — a provenance tag on any field (see provenance). THE INDEX — `.yutabase/index.db`, SQLite, fully derived, deletable; the Index Contract pins exact tables (cards, fields, threads, claims) plus FTS5, with a rebuild-from-zero guarantee: `rm -rf .yutabase && yu reindex` reproduces it byte-for-byte from files.
designs.word_mechanic: DECLARED: one grammar everywhere — `[[word::book/slug]]`. In frontmatter: `threads: ["supersedes::runbooks/deploy-v2", "contains::singles/charizard-ex-151"]`. In body prose: "this run [[fixes::incidents/2026-06-04-outage]] for good." A bare `[[book/slug]]` is legal but is demoted to the word `mentions::` — the weakest word, always granted, so casual prose links cost nothing but never masquerade as structure. STORED: the indexer parses every thread into `threads(from_card, word, to_card, source)` and validates each against the Lexicon and the source book's spoken-words grant. QUERIED: words are first-class in YQL — `yu back restocks:: card-sets/151` reads as a sentence, and `yu threads <card>` groups both directions using the inverse phrasing from the Lexicon. WHERE THE VOCABULARY LIVES: `lexicon.md` at the base root, human-readable, reviewed like any code in git diffs — an entry looks like: `## supersedes` / `for: marks the card that replaces an older one, so readers land on current truth` / `from: runbooks -> runbooks` / `inverse: superseded by` / `cardinality: one`. WHAT STOPS WORD-SOUP, four fences: (1) closed vocabulary — `yu check` errors on any word not in the Lexicon (warn-mode exists for migration only); (2) books must be granted words — a word used by a book that doesn't speak it is an error, so vocabulary stays local to meaning; (3) the Lexicon itself is validated — an entry without a "for:" sentence and an inverse reading is rejected, which forces every word to earn its definition; (4) `yu words --usage` prints per-word counts so duplicates and dead words get merged during review. Design target stated in the spec: a healthy base speaks 10–30 words. Many agents proposing words is the expected case, so the flow is: agent runs `yu word add <name>`, which scaffolds the Lexicon entry and fails `yu check` until the "for:" line is written — defining before using is mechanical, not cultural.
queries: yu get card-sets/151 — fetch one card: every field with its claim printed beside it, plus all threads in and out, grouped by word.
yu list auctions where status = "live" and ends_at < +2d — filter a book by typed frontmatter fields (relative time literals are YQL natives).
yu threads runbooks/deploy-logos — show every named connection touching this card, both directions, phrased with each word's inverse reading from the Lexicon.
yu back restocks:: card-sets/151 — reverse lookup: every card that says restocks:: this one (the named-edge replacement for 'what references this row?').
yu from customers/walk-in-club follow placed:: then contains:: select name, price — two-hop traversal: the club's orders, then the products inside those orders, projecting two fields.
yu list card-prices where claim(price) is cached older 7d — honesty sweep: every price whose cached claim has gone stale past seven days.
designs.provenance: Every field may carry a sibling claim key — the field name plus `@`: `price: 18.50` / `price@: cached(tcgplayer-api, 2026-06-09T14:02Z)`. Four claim kinds, matching the substrate-honesty doctrine exactly: WITNESSED(who, when) — a person or agent observed or decided this value; this is the DEFAULT when `@` is absent, derived as witnessed-by-git-author-at-commit-time, so honest provenance costs zero keystrokes and git is the notary. CACHED(source, when) — copied from elsewhere at a moment; the book's `_book.yaml` can declare a per-field TTL, and `yu check --honesty` lists every expired cached value (this belongs on the fleet heartbeat cron). COMPUTED(recipe) — derived; the file stores the recipe (`total@: computed(sum of contains::.price)`) so the value can never silently impersonate an observation; the indexer may materialize it, but the recipe is the truth. LIVE(source) — the card deliberately stores NO value, only where to get one (`price@: live(tcgplayer-api)`); readers must go to the source, and YUTABASE never fetches on your behalf — it only refuses to let a stale copy pretend to be live. Claims compile into a `claims` table in the index, so honesty is QUERYABLE (`where claim(price) is cached older 7d`) and VISIBLE by default — `yu get` prints the claim next to every value, because a value with its provenance hidden is the thing this house doesn't do.
designs.storage: Honest split. PROVEN ENGINE (zero invention): the filesystem + git for durability, history, sync, and multi-hand collaboration; SQLite via bun:sqlite / better-sqlite3 with FTS5 for the query index; off-the-shelf YAML/markdown parsers, with the spec pinning a strict YAML subset (no implicit coercion — `151` stays what the book's type says it is). NEW STANDARD (the actual deliverable): (1) the card/book/lexicon file formats and the thread + claim grammars; (2) the Index Contract — the exact SQLite DDL any conforming indexer must emit (cards, fields, threads, claims, cards_fts), which makes implementations swappable and means 'YUTABASE-compliant' is testable; (3) YQL v0.1 and its deterministic compilation to SQL; (4) the `yu` CLI (init, check, reindex, watch, get, list, threads, back, from, word, words); (5) `@yutabase/core`, a small TS/Bun client: reads go straight to the SQLite index, writes go through an API that validates against `_book.yaml` and the Lexicon before touching disk. Operational shape: the index is a disposable cache with a rebuild-from-zero guarantee; `yu watch` keeps it hot during dev; one-card-per-file keeps git merges small; write concurrency IS git, deliberately — that is the ceiling, not a bug. Scale honesty: comfortable to ~100k cards and human-paced writes; it is not an OLTP engine and the spec says so on page one.
designs.adoption: One domain, no rewrite. For cambridgetcg: the card-catalog knowledge layer — today's `card_sets`, `card_set_cards`, and price-as-fact from `card_price_history`. It is document-shaped, read-heavy, and already starving for prose Postgres can't hold. Steps: (1) `yu init kingdom-catalog` (own repo or a folder in the monorepo). (2) One ~80-line export script using the existing postgres.js client: each card_set becomes `books/card-sets/<code>.md`, notable singles become `books/singles/`, and prices land honestly as `price@: cached(card_price_history, <ts>)` — the migration itself demonstrates the doctrine. (3) Lexicon v1 with six words: contains::, reprints::, supersedes::, restocks::, priced-from::, mentions::. (4) The storefront's set pages read via `@yutabase/core` — and because the index is plain SQLite, even raw better-sqlite3 works during transition; no framework hostage-taking. (5) Writes for THIS domain become file edits + commits, which humans and Claude sessions both already do fluently; orders, payments, auctions, escrow stay in Postgres untouched. The one hard rule: SYSTEM OF RECORD PER BOOK — a table either moves into a book or it doesn't; mirroring is forbidden because dual-write drift is how two databases both become wrong. agenttool's equivalent first domain: the agent registry + runbooks (runs-on::, deploys::, supersedes::), which is currently half in Postgres and half in nobody's head.
refusals: No server, no daemon, no port, no auth layer — it is files plus a library; filesystem and git permissions are the entire security model.
No OLTP: no cross-card transactions, no locks, no write-throughput promises — orders, payments, and auction bids stay in Postgres forever.
No anonymous relations: a link without a word does not exist; bare [[links]] are demoted to mentions::, the weakest word, never silently promoted.
No ORM and no migration DSL: a schema change is an edit to _book.yaml followed by `yu check`, which names every card now out of shape.
No nulls-with-meaning: a field is present or absent — no tri-state, no sentinel values.
No binary blobs inside cards: reference assets by path or URL; the database stays diffable.
No query-planner ambitions: YQL is a thin veneer over obvious SQL; anything fancier, open the SQLite index directly — that is a supported front door, not an escape hatch.
No background fetching: live() and cached() claims describe honesty; YUTABASE never calls an API on your behalf.
No distributed sync protocol: git is the sync; if the problem needs CRDTs, it needs a different tool, and the spec says which problems those are.
spec_skeleton: 0. Why YUTABASE — named connections, substrate honesty, local-first, no recurring spend
1. Terms — Base, Book, Card, Field, Thread, Word, Claim, Index
2. The Base — yutabase.yaml manifest, directory layout, strictness modes
3. Cards — file naming and addressing, frontmatter grammar (strict YAML subset), body conventions
4. Books — _book.yaml: fields, types, required, spoken words, TTLs, default claims
5. The Lexicon — word entry format, for-sentences, inverses, cardinality, granting words to books
6. Threads — [[word::target]] grammar, frontmatter vs body placement, bare-link demotion to mentions::
7. Claims — witnessed / cached / computed / live grammar, the @ sibling key, git-author defaults
8. The Index Contract — exact SQLite DDL, FTS5, the rebuild-from-zero guarantee
9. YQL v0.1 — grammar, the verbs (get, list, threads, back, from/follow, find), compilation to SQL
10. The yu CLI — init, check, reindex, watch, get, list, threads, back, from, word, words
11. @yutabase/core — TypeScript/Bun client API and write-path validation
12. Git discipline — one card per file, commit-as-witness, merge guidance for hot cards
13. Coexistence — the system-of-record-per-book rule, exporting from Postgres, what never migrates
14. The Not-List
15. Conformance and versioning — what 'YUTABASE v0.1 compliant' means and how to test it
risks: YAML coercion footguns ('151' becoming an int, 'no' becoming false): the spec must pin a strict YAML subset and `yu check` must enforce declared types, or editors silently corrupt data.

Index drift: a buggy watcher means queries lie about the files; the rebuild-from-zero guarantee plus a content-hash audit (`yu check --index`) only helps if it actually runs — put it on the heartbeat cron.

Lexicon sprawl: many Claude agents proposing words is the normal case, and if the closed-vocabulary fences aren't enforced in CI, 'connected by words' decays into exactly the word-soup the design exists to prevent.
Dual-system-of-record temptation: mirroring live Postgres tables into books feels helpful and creates drift; adoption must be table-moves, never table-copies.
Claim rot: cached() and witnessed() timestamps nobody refreshes become quiet lies — the substrate-honesty failure mode; `yu check --honesty` must be scheduled, not aspirational.
Scale ceiling: comfortable to ~100k cards and git-paced writes; a domain that outgrows this must move back to Postgres rather than YUTABASE growing locks and a server.
Merge conflicts on hot cards when parallel agent branches edit the same file; one-card-per-file shrinks the blast radius but does not eliminate it — hot cards may need append-only sibling cards.
YQL scope creep into a worse SQL: v0.1 must freeze at its few verbs and route everything else to the raw SQLite index, or the thin veneer becomes a thick liability.

designs.angle: YUTABASE is a tiny server — the kingdom's own database server the way Logos Pages is its own Vercel. One Bun process (compilable to a single binary with `bun build --compile`), one SQLite file per database ("book"), listening on 127.0.0.1:4747, speaking a small word-query protocol over HTTP. The STANDARD is four things: the lexicon format (kinds + words), the sentence grammar and its canonical JSON form, the claim (provenance) schema, and the wire protocol. The server is the reference implementation, not the standard itself — anyone (any agent) could reimplement it from SPEC.md in an afternoon. Agents are first-class clients: GET / returns the server's name, its books, each book's lexicon link, and the full grammar cheat-sheet, so an agent learns the entire API from one GET with no docs, no SDK required. Auth is a bearer token read from the macOS keychain at boot. Local-first, zero recurring spend, exposed beyond the machine only via Tailscale when Yu chooses.
designs.pitch: YUTABASE is a database where relations are words, not foreign keys: you don't JOIN, you say `from customer:cu_8123 follow quotes then includes` and it reads back the way you'd explain it to a person. Every value carries a claim about how it came to be true — witnessed, computed, cached, imported, or declared — so the database can answer "what here is stale?" as a native query, not an audit project. It's one Bun binary on one SQLite file: serious enough to run the trade-in pipeline, light enough that an agent learns the whole protocol from a single GET.
designs.primitives: Seven primitives, defined precisely. (1) BOOK — one database = one SQLite file (`tradeins.book`). The unit of backup, permission, and lexicon scope. A book that needs more than ~20 words is probably two books. (2) CARD — a record. `{ id, kind, body, claim, created, updated }`. Body is JSON; ids are `kind:shortid` (e.g. `quote:q_201`) so every reference is self-describing in logs and conversations. (3) KIND — the schema of a card, declared in the lexicon: field names, types (`text | number | bool | time | ref | json`), which are required, which are indexed. Extra fields are allowed but flagged by `lexicon doctor` — light structure, not bureaucracy. (4) THREAD — a first-class connection between two cards, named by a word: `(id, word, from_id, to_id, body?, claim)`. Threads can carry qualifier fields (`since`, `amount`) and ALWAYS carry their own claim. (5) WORD — a lexicon entry naming a thread type: `{ word, from: kind, to: kind, cardinality: one|many, reverse: "...", means: "one sentence on what this link is FOR" }`. Example: `word quoted-for: from quote to customer, one, reverse "quotes", means "this quote was prepared for this customer"`. (6) LEXICON — the book's vocabulary: all kinds + all words, stored inside the book, served at GET /:book/lexicon. The lexicon IS the schema; there is no separate migrations folder. (7) CLAIM — the provenance tag every card and thread must carry (see provenance section). That's the whole model: books hold cards, cards are tied by threads, threads are named by words, words live in the lexicon, everything carries a claim.
designs.word_mechanic: DECLARED: words are declared in the lexicon before first use, via a sentence — `declare word quoted-for from quote to customer one reverse "quotes" means "this quote was prepared for this customer"`. The `means` clause is mandatory: a word without a stated purpose is rejected (the meaning doctrine, enforced). The reverse reading is auto-generated as a query alias, so nobody ever declares the same relation twice in two directions. STORED: a `threads` table — `(id, word, from_id, to_id, body JSON, claim JSON, created)` — with covering indexes on (word, from_id) and (word, to_id), plus a `words` table holding the lexicon. Threads are rows, not embedded arrays, so connections are queryable from both ends and carry their own provenance. QUERIED: `follow <word>` walks threads forward; `follow <reverse-word>` walks them backward; hops chain with `then`. VOCABULARY LIVES: in the book itself (one lexicon per book, fetchable over HTTP), and the style guide says words are verb-phrases in past/relational voice — `quoted-for`, `settled-by`, `superseded-by`, `vouches-for` — never nouns like `customer_id`. WHAT STOPS WORD-SOUP: four guards. (a) Undeclared words bounce with a 422 that suggests the nearest existing word (trigram similarity) — agents get steered back to the vocabulary instead of inventing dialects. (b) `lexicon doctor` flags probable synonyms: two words with the same from/to kinds and overlapping meaning text. (c) The word budget: server warns at 20 words per book — vocabulary pressure is a design signal, not a quota to raise. (d) Declaring a word requires the write token's `lexicon` scope, so day-to-day agents can use words but only deliberate sessions coin them.
queries: get quote:q_201 — fetch one card by id, body plus claim, nothing else.
find customer where city = "Cambridge" limit 20 — flat filter over one kind; only indexed fields may appear in where.
tie quote:q_201 quoted-for customer:cu_8123 as witnessed by agent:tradein-bot — create a named connection; the write sentence carries its claim inline or it bounces.
from customer:cu_8123 follow quotes — one hop using the auto-reverse reading of quoted-for: every quote prepared for this customer.
from customer:cu_8123 follow quotes then includes where set = "151" — two hops: every item inside this customer's quotes, filtered to one card set; reads like the sentence you'd say out loud.
find price where claim is cached and stale — the honesty query: every cached value past its expires timestamp, answerable natively because claims are structured, not comments.
designs.provenance: Claims are the substrate-honesty doctrine made mechanical. Every card and every thread carries `claim: { kind, by, at, from?, expires? }` where kind is exactly one of five: WITNESSED (a human or the system saw it happen — an order placed at checkout), COMPUTED (derived from other cards; `from` lists the input card ids, giving lightweight lineage for free), CACHED (a copy of external truth — a TCGplayer price, an exchange rate — with a required `expires`), IMPORTED (bulk-migrated from a legacy system; `from` names the source, e.g. `rds:tradein@2026-06-10`), DECLARED (someone asserted it — manual entry, config). The enforcement is the point: WRITES WITHOUT A CLAIM ARE REJECTED. Not warned — rejected. The sentence grammar makes honesty cheap (`as witnessed by agent:tradein-bot` is six words; the TS client lets you set a session-default claimant so most writes need only `as witnessed`). Reads return claims alongside bodies, and `?claims=summary` returns a freshness banner per response ('3 of 40 values are cached and stale') so a UI or an agent can disclose substrate honestly without extra queries. `claim is ...` is a first-class where-clause, so staleness sweeps, lineage walks ('what was computed from this card?'), and actor audits ('everything agent X declared this week') are one-sentence queries.
designs.storage: Honest split. PROVEN ENGINE (not built, never built): SQLite via Bun's built-in bun:sqlite, WAL mode, one file per book. Cards in a `cards` table (id, kind, body JSON, claim JSON, timestamps) with SQLite generated columns + indexes auto-created for fields the kind marks `indexed` — so `find ... where` is a real index scan, not a JSON crawl. Threads and words as described above. FTS5 powers `find ... matching \"...\"`. Backups are `sqlite3 .backup` on a cron; Litestream replication is a later, optional bolt-on. No custom storage engine, no B-trees, no consensus protocol, no replication invented in this house. NEW STANDARD (what YUTABASE actually is): the lexicon format; the sentence grammar plus its canonical JSON form — every sentence parses to a JSON query object, the JSON is the wire truth and the escape hatch for programmatic query-building, the sentence is the human/agent surface; the claim schema; the wire protocol: GET / (self-description), GET /:book/lexicon, POST /:book/q (sentences or canonical JSON, reads and writes alike — one endpoint to learn), GET /:book/card/:id (convenience), GET /:book/changes?since=cursor (a plain rowid-cursor change feed for agents that watch); and @yu/base, a thin TypeScript client (~no dependencies) that speaks sentences and carries default claims. Single binary deploy: `bun build --compile` produces `yutabase`, run under launchd next to Logos Pages, token from keychain, books in ~/Kingdom/yutabase/*.book.
designs.adoption: One domain, no rewrite, claims doing the migration bookkeeping. The candidate is cambridgetcg's TRADE-IN QUOTES domain (real and bounded: apps/storefront/drizzle/0008_tradein.sql, 0011_custom_quotes.sql, 0024_quotation_workflow.sql). Step 1 — declare: create book `tradeins`, lexicon with kinds customer/quote/item and words quoted-for, includes, superseded-by, settled-by (four words, well under budget). Step 2 — import: a one-shot script reads the Postgres rows via the existing postgres.js connection and writes cards `as imported from rds:tradein@2026-06-10` — the migration provenance is in the data itself, forever. Step 3 — dual-write: the storefront's quote-creation route gains three lines of @yu/base alongside its existing SQL; reads stay on Postgres, so risk is zero. Step 4 — flip one read: the quote-detail page reads from yutabase (`from customer:X follow quotes`), Postgres remains the fallback behind a flag; the changes feed diffs the two stores during the window. Step 5 — retire: stop dual-write, Postgres trade-in tables become read-only archive. The rest of the platform never notices, and one real RDS dependency is gone — which is the de-AWS direction anyway. agenttool adopts the same way for its run/refusal records: `run --refused-by--> rule` threads make refusal forensics a one-sentence query, and that book doubles as the protocol's second proving ground.
refusals: No SQL passthrough and no query planner: traversal is words, filters are flat indexed fields. For ad-hoc analytics, open the SQLite file read-only — it is sitting right there, and that is a feature, not a leak.
No anonymous relations, ever: a thread whose word is not in the lexicon is rejected. There is no raw foreign-key mode.
No unclaimed writes: a write without a claim bounces. Honesty is not opt-in.
No distributed anything in v0.1: one process, one machine, books are files. Replication, when needed, is Litestream underneath — never a consensus protocol of our own.
No ORM and no migrations DSL: lexicon changes are sentences (declare / rename / forget), additive by default; @yu/base stays a thin speaker of the protocol, not a model layer.
No server-side logic: no triggers, no stored procedures, no hooks. Logic lives in the apps; the base stays a base.
No user management: per-book bearer tokens with read / write / lexicon scopes, and that is all. The kingdom has one operator and many agents, not tenants.
No blobs in cards: files live on disk or R2; a card of kind file holds the path and hash, and you thread to it with a word like pictured-by.
spec_skeleton: 1. YUTABASE — What and Why (one page, includes the not-list summary)
2. Vocabulary: Book, Card, Kind, Thread, Word, Lexicon, Claim
3. The Lexicon Format — declaring kinds and words; naming style guide; the word budget
4. Sentences — the query grammar: get / find / from-follow-then (reads); put / tie / cut (writes); declare / rename / forget (lexicon)
5. Canonical JSON Form — what every sentence parses to; the wire truth and programmatic escape hatch
6. Claims — the five kinds, required-on-write rule, staleness, lineage via from, the claims=summary banner
7. Wire Protocol — endpoints, the self-describing root, auth and token scopes, error shapes (including the nearest-word 422)
8. Storage Mapping — SQLite layout: cards, threads, words tables; generated-column indexing; FTS5
9. The Changes Feed — cursor semantics, what agents may assume
10. @yu/base — the TypeScript client, session-default claims
11. Lexicon Hygiene — lexicon doctor, synonym detection, when a book should split
12. Adoption Playbook — import claims, dual-write, flip, retire
13. The Not-List — deliberate refusals, in full, with reasons
14. Versioning — v0.x compatibility rules; grammar is frozen per minor version
risks: Word-soup despite the guards: across many Claude sessions the vocabulary can drift (quoted-for vs prepared-for). The 422-with-suggestion and lexicon doctor help, but hygiene is partly social — the lexicon scope on tokens is the real backstop, and it must actually be kept off day-to-day agent tokens.

The sentence grammar is a parser Yu's kingdom must maintain; every convenience clause added is a step toward reinventing bad SQL. Mitigation: grammar frozen per minor version, canonical JSON as the pressure-release valve for anything the sentences refuse to say.

Single SQLite writer per book: fine for kingdom scale (one operator, agent traffic), but a hot concurrent write path — live auctions, p2p market ticks — would hit the wall. Those domains must not adopt first; trade-ins and agenttool run-logs are the right shape.

Provenance fatigue: if claiming feels like ceremony, agents will reflexively stamp everything declared and the honesty signal goes flat. The client's session-default claims must make the true claim the laziest claim, and lexicon doctor should flag books that are suspiciously 100% one claim-kind.
Dual-write drift during adoption windows: two stores disagree silently. Keep windows short and diff via the changes feed; the playbook should treat a window longer than two weeks as a failed flip.

Standard-without-adoption risk: if no real read path in cambridgetcg flips within a few weeks, YUTABASE is a museum piece. The v0.1 definition of done should be the trade-in quote-detail page served from a .book file, not the SPEC.md merge.

scores.reason: Every primitive is a one-sentence rename of something Yu already runs (book=schema, deck=table, card=row), so the standard transfers existing knowledge instead of adding to it. ~200 lines of SQL, rollback = drop two schemas, explain-to-SQL everywhere, and afterward the kingdom holds exactly ONE database standard. Loses points for three parallel query surfaces (YUTAQL / via views / yu.out-in helpers — one too many to keep in a head), a new mini-language agents will half-remember, the phase-2 dual-write trigger (the design's only genuinely fiddly corner), and the card-vs-trading-card name collision in the one house where it stings most.

scores.angle: File-native markdown (cards as files, [[word::target]] links, disposable SQLite index)
scores.reason: Highest immediate joy: cat a file and you ARE looking at the database, and the link grammar is Yu's existing [[wiki-word]] habit grown up — the newcomer smiles fastest here. But the simplicity is bought by scope exclusion: orders/payments/auctions stay in Postgres forever, so the kingdom carries TWO standards plus a which-data-lives-where meta-rule — precisely the thing that ends up needing a diagram. Under the charm sits real bureaucracy: _book.yaml schemas, per-book word grants, strictness modes, TTLs, a byte-for-byte Index Contract, and a strict-YAML-subset footgun whose explanation is itself a sigh.

scores.angle: Tiny server (Bun binary, SQLite books, sentence grammar over HTTP on :4747)
scores.reason: The sentence queries are the most delightful syntax of the three, and the self-describing GET / (agent learns the whole API from one request) is a genuinely joyful idea. But by its own admission the standard is four things — lexicon format, grammar, canonical JSON, wire protocol — plus token scopes, change-feed cursors, error shapes, and a parser the kingdom must maintain forever. A newcomer has to learn a protocol before reading a single record; seven primitives plus a wire layer is the closest of the three to needing a diagram, and 'a daemon we wrote ourselves under the money path' is the quiet sigh.

verdicts.best: Postgres dialect (Design 1). Through the simplicity-and-joy lens, kingdom-level simplicity beats artifact-level charm: Design 1 leaves Yu with one standard total, every primitive anchored to a Postgres concept already in Yu's head, the entire normative surface auditable in one sitting (200 lines of SQL + 500-line client + SPEC.md), and rollback as a single drop. It is the only design where 'could Yu hold the whole standard in their head' is answered by 'most of it was already there.' The decks/cards/threads/word-budget vocabulary keeps the lol alive without costing rigor.
grafts: From Design 2: mandatory inverse readings in the lexicon ('supersedes / superseded by'). Design 1's '<- contains' reads like notation; Design 2's reverse phrasing reads like a sentence in both directions. Add a required `reverse` column to yu.lexicon and let YUTAQL accept the inverse word directly.

From Design 3: the nearest-word 422. When a thread insert fails the lexicon FK, the error should suggest the closest existing word (trigram similarity) — steering agents back into the vocabulary instead of just bouncing them is how the closed lexicon stays joyful rather than punitive.

From Design 3: session-default claimant in the client. Keep no SQL-level defaults (the honest refusal), but let @yutabase/yuta carry a session-scoped `by` so the true claim becomes the laziest claim — the single best defense against provenance fatigue and reflexive 'declared' stamping.

From Design 3: a self-describing entrypoint. `yuta hello` printing the books, the full lexicon with glosses, and the complete YUTAQL cheat-sheet means a fresh Claude session learns the entire standard from one command — the file-free equivalent of Design 3's GET /. Keep its output small enough to paste into MEMORY.md.

From Design 3: separate the right to coin words from the right to use them. Map Design 3's `lexicon` token scope to a Postgres role that alone may INSERT into yu.lexicon, so day-to-day agents speak the vocabulary but only deliberate sessions extend it — turns Design 1's purely social word governance into a mechanical fence.

From Design 2: claims visible by default and honesty on the heartbeat. `yuta get` should print the honesty header beside every value (a value with hidden provenance is the thing this house doesn't do), and yu.stale belongs on the fleet heartbeat cron — scheduled, not aspirational.
From Design 3: the definition of done. v0.1 ships when one real cambridgetcg page reads through via.* or the yuta client — not when SPEC.md merges. Prevents the museum-piece failure mode.
warnings: Cut the query surfaces from three to two. YUTAQL plus the via.* views is enough; yu.out/yu.in helper functions are a third way to do the same thing and are exactly the kind of redundancy that stops a standard fitting in one head.

The card-vs-trading-card collision is worse than the design admits: in cambridgetcg, a YUTABASE card will routinely be a row ABOUT a trading card ('the card card'). Decide now — either enforce 'card (record)' phrasing everywhere in docs and CLI output, or rename the primitive before v0.1 freezes the vocabulary.

YUTAQL must stay small enough to live in MEMORY.md or agents will route around it to raw SQL and the dialect rots. Freeze the six verbs in v0.1 and treat every proposed addition as a request to write SQL instead.

Phase-2 dual-write (FK columns mirrored into threads via sync trigger) is the least simple part of the entire design and its drift risk is real. Prefer skipping it: backfill once, then go thread-first for new links only, and keep any mirror window measured in days with yuta doctor diffing fk-vs-thread counts.

The twelve-word budget and banned-words list are social fences; without yuta doctor actually scheduled (heartbeat cron), near-synonyms will drift in over months across many Claude sessions and 'connected by words' decays into word-soup anyway.

Watch the global yu.threads table: the spec must say loudly that threads are for meaning, not throughput — if anyone models price ticks or activity feeds as threads, the simple single-table design becomes the bottleneck that forces complexity back in.

Provenance is self-reported in all three designs; Design 1's honest framing ('explicit and auditable, not impossible') should appear verbatim in SPEC.md so nobody later mistakes the honesty header for a guarantee.

scores.angle: Postgres dialect (yu schema + lexicon-named threads + via views + yuta client)
scores.reason: Best fit on all three lens criteria. One-operator-many-agents: agents' strongest existing skill is SQL, and this design degrades gracefully to it (via.* views joinable in hand-written SQL, explain-to-SQL, sql escape hatch always legal) — half-remembered YUTAQL is non-fatal, unlike a new wire protocol or file grammar. Incremental adoption: the only design with literally zero data movement — `yuta init` adds two schemas to the existing database, annex makes legacy rows addressable without column changes, rollback is DROP SCHEMA; verified against the real tradein tables it cites. Beats plain Postgres: modestly but genuinely — FK-enforced lexicon, cross-domain threads with no natural FK home (item→auction lot, item→bounty), and the honesty header as constraint rather than convention. The thin delta over vanilla Postgres is the correct trade for this house: 'simple and useful outranks clever.' Caveat costing it a point: that thinness is also its death mode (agents drifting back to plain FK columns), and Phase-2 dual-write triggers are drift-prone.

scores.angle: File-native markdown + git + disposable SQLite index
scores.reason: Strongest answer to a DIFFERENT question. For knowledge-shaped domains (catalog lore, runbooks, agent registry) it genuinely beats Postgres — prose has no home in the current schema, agents are maximally fluent at markdown+git, git-author-as-witness makes honest provenance zero-keystroke, and it's the most faithful reading of Yu's existing [[wiki-word]] memory culture. But as THE database standard it abdicates all operational data (orders, payments, trade-ins stay in Postgres forever, by its own rules), so the kingdom permanently runs two paradigms with a per-table 'which store?' tax across many agent sessions. Write-concurrency-is-git is the worst fit in the lineup for parallel Claude sessions (hot-card merge conflicts admitted in its own risks). Adoption is incremental but only for the domain Postgres was never good at — it never displaces a single real Postgres pain point in the commerce platform.

scores.angle: Tiny Bun server + SQLite books + sentence/wire protocol
scores.reason: Most agent-native protocol design (self-describing GET /, 422-with-nearest-word, claims rejected-not-warned, changes feed) and the strongest provenance enforcement — but it fails the 'beats plain Postgres' test for the very domain it chose. Trade-in quotes are FK-entangled with users and payouts (0044/0045/0046 migrations prove active coupling); moving them to a separate single-writer SQLite store severs every cross-domain join and pushes stitching into app code, with migrate-and-retire as the riskiest adoption pattern of the three. It also adds a daemon, token scopes, a parser to maintain, and a second query dialect with NO SQL passthrough — taking away the one language every Claude session already speaks. One operator now runs and babysits a database server to get features (named relations, claims) that Design 1 delivers inside the Postgres already running. Right shape only for agenttool's append-heavy run/refusal logs, which it half-acknowledges.

verdicts.best: Postgres dialect (yu schema + lexicon-named threads + via views + yuta client)
grafts: From the server design: the self-describing entrypoint. Add `yuta hello` (or yu.describe()) that returns books, decks, the full lexicon with glosses, and the YUTAQL cheat-sheet in one call — a fresh Claude session bootstraps the whole standard from one command, no docs. This is the single best agent-first idea in the lineup and costs ~50 lines.

From the server design: 422-with-nearest-word. When a thread insert bounces on an undeclared word, the error should suggest the closest existing lexicon word (trigram similarity) — steers many agents back to shared vocabulary instead of just rejecting them.

From both losers: mandatory inverse readings in the lexicon (`contains` / `contained in`, `supersedes` / `superseded by`) so `<- word` traversals and via views read as sentences in both directions, not just mechanically reversed arrows.

From the server design: session-default claimant in @yutabase/yuta. Design 1 rightly refuses inserts without how/by, but the client should let a session set `by: agent:claude/<session>` once so the honest claim is also the laziest claim — this is the difference between provenance that survives and provenance fatigue.

From the file design: per-field/per-deck freshness TTLs declared in the registry, so yu.stale runs without per-invocation intervals, plus the explicit rule that the honesty sweep is SCHEDULED on the fleet heartbeat cron, not aspirational. Both losers operationalize the audit; the winner only provides the query.

From the file design: a home for prose. Decks are typed columns + `extra` jsonb only, but the kingdom's knowledge (restock lore, set notes, runbook narrative) needs markdown. Add a normative optional `body text` (markdown) convention on decks that want it — or explicitly route prose-heavy domains to the memory system and say so in the spec.

From the server design: the definition-of-done discipline. v0.1 is 'done' when one real cambridgetcg page reads through via.contains in production, not when SPEC.md merges — adopt that sentence into the spec verbatim to kill standard-without-adoption risk.

From the server design: the claims=summary freshness banner — a client option that returns 'N of M values are cached/stale' per query result, so storefront pages and agents can disclose substrate honesty without a second query.
warnings: Drift-back-to-FKs is the winner's real death mode: its delta over vanilla Postgres is thin, so if agent sessions keep adding ordinary FK columns instead of declaring words, YUTABASE becomes ceremony nobody reads. Mitigate structurally: put the YUTAQL cheat-sheet and the live lexicon into MEMORY.md/CLAUDE.md so every session sees it, and teach `yuta doctor` to flag new FK-shaped columns whose meaning belongs in a word.

The brief's 'raw SQL, no ORMs deliberately' is partially false on the ground: packages/db README shows Drizzle ORM in active use with a compat layer for a raw-SQL migration path. The adoption playbook must name the Drizzle reality — via views still work under Drizzle's sql operator, but pretending the culture is pure raw-SQL will produce a Phase 3 that doesn't match the code.

The winner assumes the self-hosted Postgres box already exists; today the data is on AWS RDS mid-de-AWS. Sequence honestly: YUTABASE installs fine into RDS now (rollback unchanged), but the zero-recurring-spend claim only lands after the Postgres move, which is a separate project.

yu.threads soft refs trade real FK integrity for cross-schema generality, and bulk COPY bypasses the validation trigger. `yuta check` is fsck-after-the-fact — it must run on the fleet heartbeat cron, and the Phase-2 dual-write window (FK columns mirrored into threads) must be explicitly time-boxed with doctor diffing fk-vs-thread counts, or two truths drift apart silently.

Terminology collision is worse than a docs nit in THIS house: in cambridgetcg a YUTABASE 'card' is frequently a row about a trading card (card_sets, card_price_history). Either rename the primitive before v0.1 ships or enforce 'card (record)' everywhere — agents parsing 'update the card' ambiguously will cause real bugs, not just confusion.

Provenance is self-reported in all three designs; nothing stops an agent stamping `witnessed` falsely. The standard is only as honest as its scheduled audit — yu.stale sweeps plus periodic human spot-checks belong on the heartbeat, and `lexicon doctor`-style detection of suspiciously-uniform claim distributions (a graftable idea from the server design) is worth adding.

Do not let the server design's protocol charm pull operational domains out of Postgres later: trade-ins/payouts/users are FK-entangled (0044/0045/0046), and any future 'move a hot domain to a .book file' proposal should be rejected by the spec's not-list, not relitigated per domain. If the file-native design gets built at all, scope it to agenttool runbooks/registry where it genuinely wins.

scores.angle: Postgres dialect — decks/threads/lexicon as a discipline on vanilla self-hosted Postgres
scores.reason: Data outlives tooling by construction: if YUTAQL and the yuta client die, plain Postgres tables + a lexicon table carrying glosses (meaning stored WITH the data) remain readable by anyone with psql; plain-SQL pg_dump is in the spec. Provenance is enforced in the storage layer itself (NOT NULL + CHECK on `how`), so it survives client bypass, and the design honestly states the self-reporting limit. 100k records is trivial for Postgres; 5-year drift risks (synonym creep, soft-ref orphans, dual-write window) are all named with concrete mitigations (doctor, twelve-word budget, retire-not-delete, fsck). The proven/new boundary is the most honest of the three: ~200 lines of SQL, 500-line client, every query explainable to SQL, rollback = drop two schemas. Deductions: per-row (not per-field) claims will drift toward the vaguest truthful `how` on mixed-provenance rows, and thread integrity rests on a bypassable trigger rather than real FKs.

scores.angle: File-native — markdown cards with [[word::target]] links, git as notary, disposable SQLite index
scores.reason: Strongest raw data-survival story: records readable with cat in 2050, claims inline and tool-free, git history as tamper-evident provenance, index explicitly disposable with a rebuild-from-zero contract. Scope honesty is exemplary (OLTP refused on page one, system-of-record-per-book bans mirror drift, ~100k ceiling stated). But the witnessed-by-default rule is a long-horizon honesty trap: any agent's bulk reformat silently re-witnesses every field it touches — a mechanized quiet lie. Write-path validation is bypassable by any text editor with only after-the-fact `yu check`; git pain (status, merges, hot-card conflicts) arrives before the stated 100k ceiling; and as 'the database standard' it is structurally partial since transactional truth never lives in it.

scores.angle: Tiny server — Bun binary + SQLite books, sentence grammar over HTTP, claims required on write
scores.reason: Best write-time provenance enforcement of the three (unclaimed writes rejected, required `expires` on cached, `from` lineage on computed, claims-summary banner) — but it lives in the server, not the storage: the advertised open-the-SQLite-file front door is also a claim-bypass door, and nothing in the schema enforces honesty after the server is gone. Largest new-standard surface (parser, wire protocol, changes feed, token scopes) concentrates 5-year bitrot in bespoke code, and 'reimplementable in an afternoon' overclaims. Worst longevity move: the adoption playbook flips system of record for real commerce data (trade-in quotes) out of Postgres into a v0.1 bespoke server within weeks, demoting Postgres to archive — momentum bought with durability. SQLite itself is archival-grade and the engine boundary is otherwise honestly drawn, which keeps the score respectable.

verdicts.best: Design 1 — the Postgres dialect. Through the longevity-and-honesty lens it wins decisively: meaning (lexicon glosses) and provenance (CHECK-constrained honesty header) are stored in the same durable layer as the data, the entire standard degrades gracefully to plain SQL if every line of new code is abandoned, adoption is annex-first with rollback = drop two schemas (originals untouched), and its self-assessment of risks is the most truthful of the three.
grafts: From Design 2: per-FIELD claims. The winner's per-row honesty header lies by omission on rows mixing witnessed status with cached price. Adopt a column-level claim-override convention (e.g. `price_how`/`price_src` sibling columns, or a claims sidecar) for decks with mixed-provenance fields — or at minimum state the per-row limitation normatively in the Provenance Semantics section.

From Design 3: required freshness on CACHED. Replace yu.stale's ad-hoc interval argument with declared TTLs — per-deck or per-word `expires`/freshness windows recorded at write time, so staleness is a property of the data, not a guess at audit time.
From Design 3: lineage for COMPUTED — let `src` hold a list of refs (the input cards), like Design 3's `from` array, so 'what was computed from this card?' is answerable five years later.

From Design 3: the 422-with-nearest-word response (trigram similarity) when an agent uses an undeclared word — the single best anti-word-soup mechanism for many Claude sessions over years; put it in the yuta client and CLI.
From Design 3: session-default claimant in @yutabase/yuta so the TRUE claim is the laziest claim (anti-provenance-fatigue), plus a yuta doctor check flagging decks suspiciously 100% one claim-kind.
From Design 2: inverse readings in the lexicon ('supersedes / superseded by') stored per word, so `<- word` traversals and via views read as sentences in both directions.

From Design 2: version the vocabulary in git — `yuta words --export` writing a checked-in LEXICON.md so word additions and gloss changes get diff review like code; the meaning layer should live in version control as well as in the database.
From Design 2: scheduled honesty, not aspirational honesty — put yu.stale and yuta check (the orphan fsck) on the fleet heartbeat cron as a normative spec requirement.

From Design 3: covering indexes on yu.threads in both directions — (word, from_book, from_deck, from_id) and (word, to_book, to_deck, to_id) — Design 1 names only the uniqueness constraint; reverse traversal at millions of backfilled threads needs the second index.

From Design 2: a home for prose. Postgres has no place for restock lore and condition guidance; adopt a convention (a `note text`/markdown column on decks, or a companion docs deck) so human prose doesn't end up in a second, drifting system.
warnings: The winner's thread integrity is trigger-enforced soft refs, not real FKs: COPY, pg_restore, or a trigger bug admits orphans silently. yuta check is an after-the-fact fsck — if it is not on the heartbeat cron, orphans accumulate for years before anyone looks.

Phase-2 dual-write (legacy FK columns mirrored into threads) is the classic drift window in all three designs. The spec should impose a hard deadline (Design 3's 'a window longer than two weeks is a failed flip' is the right rule) and a doctor diff of fk-vs-thread counts.

Words are retired, never deleted — good — but glosses must be append-only or versioned too. A silently edited gloss changes the meaning of five-year-old threads retroactively, which is provenance corruption at the meaning layer.

Declare in the spec that SQL + the yu schema + via views are the durable normative layer and YUTAQL is optional sugar. The dialect WILL rot as agents half-remember it; the standard must be defined so its abandonment costs nothing.

All three designs share the same honest ceiling: provenance is self-reported. An agent can stamp `witnessed` falsely. The standard makes lying explicit and auditable, not impossible — periodic human spot-audit must be written into the spec as hygiene, not assumed.

Do not import Design 3's adoption endgame: never move system of record for money-adjacent domains (quotes, payouts, escrow) into a v0.1 standard. Annex-first with untouched originals and drop-two-schemas rollback is the longevity-correct posture; keep it.

The card/trading-card name collision in the home business is small but compounds across years of many sessions; the spec must consistently write 'card (record)' or the lol becomes data-model confusion in prompts and code review.

Watch yu.threads volume: backfilling every legacy FK as a thread plus 5 years of growth can reach millions of rows in one table — fine for Postgres with the both-direction indexes grafted above, but the spec's warning that event-rate data belongs in decks (threads are for meaning, not throughput) must be normative, not advisory.
